status_notes: Initial research from AWS VPC documentation, 2026-04-04. Pass 1 (service docs). Needs Pass 2 (API), Pass 3 (cross-reference), and Pass 5 (human review with live AWS environment).
notes: Foundation networking service. Provides network isolation, traffic filtering, and private connectivity for all AWS compute resources.

data.description: Stateful firewall rules at the instance/ENI level. Rules define allowed inbound and outbound traffic by protocol, port range, and source/destination (CIDR, security group, or prefix list). Stateful means return traffic is automatically allowed. Up to 5 security groups per ENI.

options.description: Allow traffic by protocol/port/source. Evaluated as union (any rule match allows).
Allow traffic by protocol/port/destination. Evaluated as union.
Security group references enable dynamic membership-based rules

default.value: Deny all inbound, allow all outbound
default.note: New security groups deny all inbound traffic and allow all outbound traffic by default.

data.vendor_recommendation: Follow least-privilege — open only required ports to required sources. Use security group references instead of CIDR blocks where possible for dynamic, maintenance-free rules.

data.description: Stateless firewall rules at the subnet level. Rules are evaluated by priority number (lowest first). Both inbound and outbound rules must explicitly allow traffic (stateless — return traffic is not automatically allowed). Each subnet is associated with exactly one NACL.

options.description: Lower number = higher priority. First matching rule wins.
Explicit allow or deny per rule
TCP, UDP, ICMP, or protocol number
Must explicitly allow ephemeral port range (1024-65535) for return traffic due to stateless nature

default.note: Default NACL allows all traffic. Custom NACLs deny all traffic by default.

data.vendor_recommendation: Use NACLs as a secondary layer of defense alongside security groups. NACLs are most useful for explicitly denying specific IP ranges (e.g., known malicious IPs).

configure.cli: aws ec2 create-network-acl-entry --network-acl-id {acl-id} --rule-number 100 --protocol tcp --port-range From=443,To=443 --cidr-block 0.0.0.0/0 --rule-action allow --ingress

match_nodes.description: Capture IP traffic to/from network interfaces

data.description: Capture information about IP traffic going to and from network interfaces. Can be applied at VPC level (all ENIs), subnet level, or individual ENI level. Captures metadata (source/dest IP, ports, protocol, packets, bytes, action) — not packet contents.

options.description: Level at which traffic is captured
Where flow log records are published
Select specific fields to include in log records for cost optimization

default.note: Flow logs are not enabled by default. Must be explicitly created.

data.vendor_recommendation: Enable VPC flow logs for all VPCs. Use S3 for cost-effective long-term storage. Use CloudWatch Logs for real-time monitoring and alerting. Use custom log format to capture only needed fields.

configure.cli: aws ec2 create-flow-logs --resource-type VPC --resource-ids {vpc-id} --traffic-type ALL --log-destination-type s3 --log-destination {s3-arn}

match_nodes.description: Private connectivity to AWS services without internet

data.description: Enable private connectivity between your VPC and supported AWS services without requiring an internet gateway, NAT gateway, VPN connection, or Direct Connect. Traffic stays on the Amazon network.

options.description: Route-table based. S3 and DynamoDB only. Free. No ENI created.
ENI with private IP in your subnet. Most AWS services. Hourly + per-GB charges.

data.vendor_recommendation: Use gateway endpoints for S3 and DynamoDB (free, no configuration overhead). Use interface endpoints for other services when resources are in private subnets. Apply endpoint policies to restrict which operations are allowed through the endpoint.

data.description: Subnets whose route table has no route to an internet gateway. Resources in private subnets have no direct inbound or outbound internet access. Outbound internet access can be provided via NAT gateway if needed.
data.vendor_recommendation: Put sensitive workloads in private subnets. Use private subnets as the default for databases, application servers, and internal services. Only place resources in public subnets when they must be directly internet-accessible (load balancers, bastion hosts).

audit.note: A subnet is 'private' if its associated route table has no route to an internet gateway (igw-*).

match_nodes.description: Allow private subnet resources to reach internet for outbound only

data.description: Managed NAT service that enables resources in private subnets to initiate outbound connections to the internet while preventing unsolicited inbound connections from the internet.

options.description: Provides internet access to private subnets via an Elastic IP. Placed in a public subnet.
Enables communication between VPCs or on-premises networks without internet access. No Elastic IP.

data.vendor_recommendation: Use NAT gateways for private subnet internet access instead of NAT instances. Deploy in multiple AZs for high availability. Monitor with CloudWatch metrics (BytesOut, ConnectionAttemptCount, ErrorPortAllocation).

data.description: Horizontally scaled, redundant, highly available VPC component that allows communication between your VPC and the internet. One internet gateway per VPC. Provides NAT for instances with public IPv4 addresses.
data.vendor_recommendation: Use only for public-facing resources. Pair with security groups and NACLs to control traffic. Most workloads should be in private subnets behind a NAT gateway or load balancer, not directly exposed via an internet gateway.

data.description: Networking connection between two VPCs that enables routing traffic between them using private IPv4/IPv6 addresses. Non-transitive — VPC A peered with VPC B and VPC B peered with VPC C does not mean VPC A can reach VPC C. Works cross-account and cross-region.

match_nodes.description: Central hub for VPC, VPN, Direct Connect routing

data.description: Regional network transit hub that interconnects VPCs, VPN connections, and Direct Connect gateways. Transitive routing — any attached network can communicate with any other. Scales to thousands of VPCs. Supports route tables for network segmentation.
data.vendor_recommendation: Use transit gateway instead of mesh VPC peering for complex network topologies. Use route table segmentation to isolate workloads (e.g., production vs development, shared services vs application VPCs).

match_nodes.description: VPC DNS resolution and hostname settings

data.description: Controls whether the VPC supports DNS resolution and DNS hostnames. enableDnsSupport enables the Amazon-provided DNS server (AmazonProvidedDNS at VPC CIDR + 2). enableDnsHostnames assigns public DNS hostnames to instances with public IPs.

options.description: Enables DNS resolution via the Amazon-provided DNS server

options.default: true for default VPC, false for non-default VPCs
options.description: Assigns public DNS hostnames to instances with public IPs

data.vendor_recommendation: Keep enableDnsSupport enabled. Enable enableDnsHostnames if using VPC endpoints (required for interface endpoints to work with private DNS). Use Route 53 Resolver for hybrid DNS resolution.

match_nodes.description: Auto-created security group in every VPC

data.description: Every VPC has a default security group that cannot be deleted. Instances launched without specifying a security group are assigned to the default security group. Default rules allow all inbound traffic from instances assigned to the same default security group and allow all outbound traffic.

default.value: Allow all inbound from same group, allow all outbound
default.note: Default rules are permissive within the group.

data.vendor_recommendation: Restrict default security group rules — many compliance benchmarks (CIS AWS Foundations) require that the default security group has no inbound or outbound rules, ensuring instances must be explicitly assigned to a purpose-built security group.

configure.note: Cannot delete the default security group — can only modify its rules to be restrictive.
